explicit_text: I'm recording this interview here in the office of Voices of Oklahoma in Tulsa. It's a great organization that preserves stories like mine for future generations. Being part of Voices of Oklahoma lets me share my life experiences from Claremore and beyond. It’s a meaningful way to keep Oklahoma’s history alive.

explicit_text: I live here in Claremore, Oklahoma, on a little farm where I have my office. After my family moved back from California when I was an infant, Claremore became home. I went to school around here, including Claremore High School, and worked on different farms nearby. Claremore has been the center of my life for many years.

I went to school around here, including the local high school, and worked on different farms nearby. This place has been the center of my life for many years.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic kept me home a lot, especially with the snowstorm we had recently. It shut down my commercial jobs, which affected my livelihood, but I managed to stock up and had friends help me with shopping. I was scheduled for vaccination a couple of times but had cancellations. It definitely changed how I moved around but didn’t stop me from staying active in other ways.

explicit_text: I was born in Emmett, California, specifically in the Baker's Street labor camp. My parents were migrant workers picking grapes and doing whatever they could to survive during the Great Depression era. Though I was only four months old when we returned to Oklahoma, Emmett is where my earliest story begins, in that labor camp setting.
implicit_text: I was born in a small California town, specifically in a labor camp on Baker's Street. My parents were migrant workers picking grapes and doing whatever they could to survive during the Great Depression era. Though I was only four months old when we returned to Oklahoma, that town is where my earliest story begins, in that labor camp setting.

explicit_text: The Baker's Street labor camp in California was where I was born. It was a place where migrant farm workers lived in tents and makeshift housing while picking grapes and other crops. It reminded me of the scenes from The Grapes of Wrath. On my 50th birthday, I went back to see the site, though it was no longer there except for a grocery store and parking lot.

On my 50th birthday, I went back to see the site, though it was no longer there except for a grocery store and parking lot.

explicit_text: My mother, Beatrice May Stoneburger, grew up in Gore, Oklahoma. She was uneducated but a very hard worker who raised six of us to adulthood despite many pregnancies. Sadly, she passed away when I was only nine years old. Her strength and work ethic left a lasting impression on me.
implicit_text: My mother grew up in a small town in Oklahoma. She was uneducated but a very hard worker who raised six of us to adulthood despite many pregnancies. Sadly, she passed away when I was only nine years old. Her strength and work ethic left a lasting impression on me.

explicit_text: Gore, Oklahoma is where my mother, Beatrice May Stoneburger, grew up. It's close to Braggs, where my father was from. Though I didn't grow up there myself, Gore is part of my family roots and heritage. It’s a small town near where my parents lived before moving around Oklahoma and eventually to California.
implicit_text: That small Oklahoma town is where my mother grew up. It's close to another small town where my father was from. Though I didn't grow up there myself, it is part of my family roots and heritage. It’s a small town near where my parents lived before moving around Oklahoma and eventually to California.

explicit_text: My father, Walter Kitt Bingham, was born in 1901 in Indian Territory near Braggs, Oklahoma. He was a jack-of-all-trades: a good fiddle player, a moonshine maker, a painter, and a barber. He was arrested several times for making illegal whiskey, which was a big part of how he made a living. Unfortunately, he died at 53 from cirrhosis of the liver.
implicit_text: My father was born in 1901 in what was then called Indian Territory near a small town in Oklahoma. He was a jack-of-all-trades: a good fiddle player, a moonshine maker, a painter, and a barber. He was arrested several times for making illegal whiskey, which was a big part of how he made a living. Unfortunately, he died at 53 from cirrhosis of the liver.

explicit_text: Braggs, Oklahoma is where my father, Walter Kitt Bingham, was from, close to Gore where my mother grew up. It’s part of the area in eastern Oklahoma where my family roots are strong. My dad stayed in that region for a long time, especially because of the water sources he needed for making moonshine. Though I didn’t live there long, it’s an important place in my family history.

explicit_text: My father was born in 1901 in Indian Territory, which is what Oklahoma was called before statehood. That was a time when the area was still transitioning and had many Native American lands. It’s part of our family history and shows how long my family has been in this region. Indian Territory shaped much of the culture and life my parents experienced.

explicit_text: My dad worked on a WPA project putting in a water line from Spavanaugh to Tulsa, earning a dollar a day. That was during the Great Depression when the government created jobs to help people survive. After that project ended, my family moved to California to follow harvest work. The WPA job was one of the few steady incomes they had during tough times.

That was during the Great Depression when the government created jobs to help people survive. After that project ended, my family moved to California to follow harvest work. The job was one of the few steady incomes they had during tough times.

explicit_text: The Grapes of Wrath is a story that really connects with my family’s experience. My parents made the same trip to California as the Joad family did in the book and movie. The labor camp where I was born resembled the migrant camps depicted in The Grapes of Wrath. On my 50th birthday, I visited the site to see what it was like, though it had changed a lot by then.

explicit_text: I attended Chambers School, which was about a mile from where I live now, three miles south of Claremore. It was a one-room schoolhouse with eight grades all taught together by Mrs. Lawrence, a wonderful teacher. I loved that school even though we moved around a lot. It was part of the old Oklahoma tradition of small, local schools close to home.
implicit_text: I attended a one-room schoolhouse about a mile from where I live now, three miles south of Claremore. It had eight grades all taught together by Mrs. Lawrence, a wonderful teacher. I loved that school even though we moved around a lot. It was part of the old Oklahoma tradition of small, local schools close to home.

explicit_text: Lone Elm School was one of the places I attended as we moved around the Claremore area. It was a bit more modern than Chambers School and had running water and other amenities. We went back and forth between schools like Lone Elm, Verdigris, and Justice depending on where we lived. These schools were all part of my early education in Oklahoma.

explicit_text: Verdigris, Oklahoma was another place where I went to school for a while. It was more modern than some of the one-room schools, with running water and better facilities. My family moved around a lot, so I attended schools in Verdigris, Lone Elm, and Justice before settling back near Claremore. Verdigris was part of my childhood schooling experience.

explicit_text: I graduated from Justice School after completing eighth grade. It was one of the small schools in the Claremore area where I spent part of my childhood. There were only eight students in my class, and I still have my diploma from there. Justice School was a stepping stone before I went on to high school in Claremore.

explicit_text: I attended Claremore High School for part of my high school years, though I also spent a year at Collinsville High. I left school in the 11th grade to join the Air Force in 1955. Claremore High was where I got much of my formal education before starting my adult life. It was a typical small-town high school experience in Oklahoma.

It was part of the back-and-forth moving my family did during those years. Collinsville was just another small town in the area where I continued my education before leaving school to join the Air Force.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Air Force on August 3rd, 1955, right after leaving school in the 11th grade. There were five of us from Claremore who joined together. After basic training, we were scattered all over the country. President Eisenhower was in office, and since there was no war, I was discharged after two years instead of four due to hardship to help my younger siblings.
implicit_text: I joined the Air Force on August 3rd, 1955, right after leaving school in the 11th grade. There were five of us from Claremore who joined together. After basic training, we were scattered all over the country. The president was Eisenhower, and since there was no war, I was discharged after two years instead of four due to hardship to help my younger siblings.

explicit_text: Dwight D. Eisenhower was president when I joined the Air Force in 1955. Because there was no active war, the military was overstocked and offered early discharge options. I took advantage of that and was discharged after two years instead of four to help my family. Eisenhower’s presidency shaped the military environment during my service.
implicit_text: The president at the time I joined the Air Force in 1955 was Eisenhower. Because there was no active war, the military was overstocked and offered early discharge options. I took advantage of that and was discharged after two years instead of four to help my family. His presidency shaped the military environment during my service.

explicit_text: Pryor, Oklahoma had an orphanage where I could have lived after my mother died, but I chose not to go there. Instead, I lived with seven different foster families, mostly on dairy farms. Pryor was part of the welfare system that helped place children like me when families couldn’t care for us. It’s a place connected to a difficult time in my childhood.

It’s a place connected to a difficult time in my childhood.

explicit_text: Leroy Van Dyke has been a close friend for many years; he calls me almost every morning without fail. Even when he had a hip replacement or was out in Wyoming with no phone service, he’d call as soon as he could. We share stories and enjoy each other’s company, and I never get tired of hearing his tales. He’s a big part of my daily routine and friendship circle.

We share stories and enjoy each other’s company, and I never get tired of hearing his tales. He’s a big part of my daily routine and friendship circle.

explicit_text: I know Leroy Van Dyke from his famous song 'The Auctioneer.' It’s part of his legacy and how many people recognize him. His music career has been impressive, and he still works many days a year performing. Our friendship goes beyond his music, but 'The Auctioneer' is definitely a highlight of his career.
